4: vesomach yado al rosh haolah veratzon lo lekapper alav: 'He shall lay his hand on the head of the burnt offering, and it shall be accepted for him to make atonement for him.' The semikah (laying of hands) is the key act of self-identification with the sacrifice: the offerer transfers his identity to the animal, which then represents him before YHWH. The term kopher (ransom/atonement, verb kipper) has its root either in 'to cover' or 'to ransom/substitute' (the latter supported by the Semitic cognate). The NT takes both senses: Christ's blood covers/forgives sin (Col 2:13-14) and he gives his life as a ransom (lytron) for many (Mark 10:45).
1: Vayiqra el-Moshe (vayiqra): 'And he called to Moses.' The book's Hebrew title is Vayiqra from this opening word (Greek Leuitikon = 'Levitical things'). The divine calling from the tent of meeting (ohel mo'ed) is not a new revelation but a continuation of Sinai: YHWH now speaks from within the tabernacle just completed. The shift from Sinai's mountain to the tent marks the portability of the covenant — the holy space moves with the people.
2: adam ki yaqriv mikem qorban la-YHWH (qorban, H7133): 'When any person among you brings a qorban to YHWH.' The word qorban derives from qarav (to draw near, to approach) — an offering is fundamentally an act of approach, not appeasement. Adam (a person — the generic human term, from adamah = ground) signals the universal scope: any person, not just a priest or official, may bring an offering. The democratization of access to YHWH is built into the vocabulary of sacrifice.
3: 'olah (H5930): 'burnt offering.' From alah (to go up, to ascend): the 'olah is the entirely ascending sacrifice — every part goes up in smoke to YHWH, nothing returned to the offerer. Tamim (H8549): 'without defect, blameless' — the same term used of Noah (Gen 6:9 'Noah was tamim') and Abraham (Gen 17:1 'walk before me and be tamim'). Ratzon (H7522): acceptance, favor, goodwill — the sacrifice is presented 'so that it may be accepted for him' (lirtsono). The 'olah is the offering of total dedication: the whole self, symbolized by the whole animal, going up to God.
5: shachat (H7819) 'to slaughter' + zaraq (H2236) 'to sprinkle/toss': the two key sacrificial actions distinct from one another — killing and blood-application are separate ritual acts performed by separate parties (the offerer kills; the priests handle the blood). Dam (H1818) 'blood': its application around the altar (al ha-mizbeach saviv) will be explained theologically in Lev 17:11 — 'the life (nefesh) of the flesh is in the blood, and I have given it to you on the altar to make atonement for your souls.'
6: hifshit et ha-'olah venitteach otah linetachehah: 'He skins the burnt offering and cuts it into pieces.' Nittach (H5408): to cut into specified pieces — the animal is not burned whole but dismembered into portions. This contrasts with the bird offering (vv. 14-17) which is not cut apart. The skinning and portioning is the offerer's work; the arrangement and burning belong to the priests. The labor division reflects the collaborative nature of the sacrifice: offerer and priest each have required roles.
7: venatan bene Aharon ha-kohen esh al ha-mizbeach: Aaron's sons arrange fire on the altar and wood upon the fire. The altar fire is divinely kindled (Lev 9:24 fire came out from before YHWH) and must never go out (Lev 6:13 'esh tamid' = perpetual fire). The priests' role in arranging wood ensures the fire is maintained through human stewardship of what God initiated. The combination of divine origin and human maintenance is characteristic of covenant institutions.
8: rosh (H7218) 'head' + peder (H6309) 'the fat pieces/suet': the head and suet are arranged on the burning wood in a specific order (ve'archu = 'they arrange in order'). The technical precision of the ritual ordering reflects its communicative function: a properly arranged sacrifice speaks; an improperly arranged one does not. The priests as arranged-order specialists is what makes the sacrificial system communicative rather than merely transactional.
9: qerev (H7130) 'inner parts/entrails' and karaim (H3767) 'legs': washed with water before burning. The washing of the internal organs and legs addresses the parts most visibly soiled — the contents of the gut and the dirt of travel. Even in sacrifice, ritual purity is addressed before the offering ascends. Reah nihoah (H7381+H5207): 'soothing/pleasing aroma' — a metaphorical idiom for what satisfies the divine response. The term nihoah derives from nuach (to rest, settle) — the aroma is what causes YHWH's response to settle/rest from potential judgment. Used first of Noah (Gen 8:21), where the same phrase appears after the flood.
10: tzon (H6629): 'flock' — sheep or goats. The graded sacrificial system moves from large animals (herd, v.2) to small animals (flock, v.10) to birds (v.14), calibrated to the offerer's means. This graduated access ensures that no one is excluded from the 'olah by economic constraint. The same animal standard applies regardless of socioeconomic class: tamim zachar (a blameless male) across all categories.
11: ve'shachat oto al yerekh ha-mizbeach tzafonah: slaughter on the north side of the altar. The north-side specification for the 'olah and later the chattat (Lev 4:24, 6:25) is never explicitly explained in the text. Rabbinic tradition associated the north with divine judgment (cf. Jer 1:14 'from the north disaster will be poured out'); this may encode the direction of sacrificial atonement. The spatial precision of the ritual — specific sides, specific movements — maps the tabernacle as a geography of meaning.
12: linetiachehah: into its pieces — repeated from v.6 to confirm the cutting procedure applies to flock animals as well as cattle. The portions specified: head, fat (peder), and remaining pieces (netachim). The arrangement is identical regardless of the animal's size — the ritual form is stable even as the material scales down. Consistency of form across economic categories reinforces that the significance is in the act, not the magnitude of the gift.
13: qerev vekaraim (inner organs and legs) again washed with water — repeated from v.9 for flock animals. The refrain of washing before burning and the formula reah nihoah la-YHWH marks the conclusion of each variant: the outcome is the same regardless of the animal used. The liturgical repetition is not redundancy but reinforcement: the system of access is genuinely available at all levels.
14: 'olah min ha-'of ('of, H5775): 'burnt offering from birds (turtledove or young pigeon).' Tor (H8449, turtledove) and ben-yonah (H1469, young pigeon) are the most economical sacrifice possible — the provision for the very poor. Lev 12:8 allows birds as an alternative to a lamb for new mothers who cannot afford a sheep; Luke 2:24 records that Mary and Joseph brought 'a pair of turtledoves or two young pigeons' — the offering of those without means, identifying Jesus with the economically poor from birth.
15: malaph et rosho (malaph, H4454): 'to wring off the head' — a specific technique for bird sacrifice distinct from the knife-slaughter used for mammals. The blood is drained against the side of the altar (himatzer). Birds are not skinned or portioned but prepared whole. The unique method for bird sacrifice — itself a unique provision for those who cannot afford animals — shows that the system is adapted to the offerer's condition at every level, not applied uniformly regardless of context.
16: muria (H4239): the crop/craw (digestive organ of the bird); notzato (H5133): feathers or wing-tips. These are removed and thrown by the altar at the ash heap on the east side (miqom ha-deshen). The bird's digestive contents (which would be unclean) are discarded before the offering ascends. The separation of the digestible interior from the ascent portion maintains the purity of what goes up to YHWH even in the least expensive offering category.
17: shissa oto vikhnafav: 'He splits it at its wings without dividing it completely.' Unlike animal offerings, the bird is not cut apart — it is torn open at the wings and spread flat for burning. The result is a flattened, opened bird laid on the altar wood. Reah nihoah la-YHWH closes the chapter: regardless of whether the offering is a bull or a pigeon, the formula is the same. The theological outcome of the sacrifice — YHWH's soothing response — is not indexed to the economic value of the offering.
